questions.stem: What makes a sequence of steps qualify as an 'algorithm'?
options: It must be written in a programming language
It must be finite (terminates), deterministic (same input → same output), and correct (solves the stated problem for all valid inputs)
It must use at least one loop and one conditional
It must be more than 10 steps long to count as an algorithm
questions.explanation: An algorithm has three requirements: (1) Finite — it must stop eventually (an infinite loop is not an algorithm); (2) Deterministic — same input always gives same output; (3) Correct — it actually solves the problem. Gradient descent is an algorithm: it terminates (after N steps), is deterministic (given same weights and data), and minimises the loss. 'Find the best neural architecture' is NOT an algorithm — it's too vague.

questions.stem: Gradient descent is the most important algorithm in machine learning. What problem does it solve?
options: Finding the global maximum of any function
Iteratively adjusting model parameters in the direction that reduces the loss function — minimising prediction error by taking small steps opposite to the gradient
Sorting training examples by difficulty before feeding them to the model
Selecting which features to include in the model
questions.explanation: Given a loss function L(weights), gradient descent computes the gradient ∇L (the direction of steepest increase) and updates: weights = weights - lr × ∇L. Moving opposite to the gradient reduces the loss. SGD, Adam, AdamW are all variants. 'Gradient' means derivative with respect to each weight — backpropagation computes these efficiently via the chain rule. The learning rate controls step size.

questions.stem: What is the difference between an algorithm and a heuristic?
options: A heuristic is written in pseudocode; an algorithm is in code
An algorithm guarantees a correct solution; a heuristic is a rule-of-thumb that usually works well but has no optimality guarantee
Heuristics run faster; algorithms are more accurate
Algorithms are for sorting; heuristics are for searching
questions.explanation: Dijkstra's shortest path algorithm guarantees the optimal path. Beam search is a heuristic — it finds a good path through token sequences but not necessarily the best one. Many ML 'algorithms' are actually heuristics: Adam's adaptive learning rates don't guarantee convergence; dropout doesn't guarantee generalisation. Knowing this distinction helps: when exact solutions are intractable (NP-hard), well-designed heuristics are the engineering answer.

questions.stem: What is binary search and what is its key precondition?
options: A search algorithm that tries two random positions and picks the better one
Repeatedly halving the search space by comparing the target to the middle element — requires the array to be SORTED; achieves O(log n) comparisons
Searching through a binary tree data structure
A search that checks only even-indexed elements, then odd-indexed elements
questions.explanation: Binary search: compare target to arr[mid]. If target < arr[mid], search left half; if target > arr[mid], search right half. Halve the search space each step: 1M elements → 20 comparisons (log₂(1,000,000) ≈ 20). REQUIRES sorted array — won't work otherwise. Used in: vocabulary token lookup (sorted vocab), hyperparameter search (bisection), finding boundaries in sorted prediction scores.

questions.stem: What is dynamic programming and how does it differ from brute-force recursion?
options: Dynamic programming uses while loops instead of recursion
Dynamic programming caches (memoises) solutions to overlapping subproblems, converting exponential time recursion to polynomial time — solves each subproblem exactly once
Dynamic programming changes the algorithm based on data size
Dynamic programming is a way to train neural networks dynamically
questions.explanation: Naive Fibonacci: fib(n) calls fib(n-1) and fib(n-2), each re-computing fib(n-2) twice — exponential O(2ⁿ). With memoisation: @lru_cache(None); each fib(k) computed once — O(n). This is DP. In ML: Viterbi algorithm (sequence labelling), edit distance (text similarity for deduplication), CTC loss (speech recognition) all use DP. The key insight: identify overlapping subproblems, cache their results.

questions.stem: Beam search is used to generate text from language models. What trade-off does the beam width k control?
options: k controls the vocabulary size used during generation
k controls the number of candidate sequences kept at each step — larger k explores more paths (better quality, slower) vs k=1 (greedy, fastest, often lower quality)
k controls the maximum length of the generated sequence
k controls the temperature of the softmax during generation
questions.explanation: Greedy decoding (k=1): always pick the most probable next token. Good for speed, often suboptimal. Beam search keeps the top-k partial sequences at each step, expanding all k candidates to pick the new top-k. k=4: 4× more compute but explores paths that start with non-optimal tokens (which may lead to better completions). k=1 is greedy; k=∞ is exhaustive (infeasible). k=4-8 is standard for machine translation.
